questions.q: A Lie group is best described as:
choices: a finite group together with a topology
a smooth manifold that is also a group, with multiplication and inversion smooth
any topological space with a continuous group law
a vector space equipped with a bilinear bracket
questions.explain: A Lie group is simultaneously a smooth manifold and a group, where the multiplication $\mu:G\times G\to G$ and inversion $\iota:G\to G$ are smooth maps.

questions.q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$?
questions.explain: $\mathrm{SU}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2-1$. For $n=2$ this gives $3$, consistent with $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$.

questions.q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ for $n=4$?
questions.explain: $\dim\mathrm{SO}(n)=\binom{n}{2}=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$. For $n=4$: $\binom{4}{2}=6$.

hard.q: Which of the following is NOT a Lie group?
choices: $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$, the invertible $n\times n$ real matrices
$\mathrm{SL}_n(\mathbb{R})$, the matrices with determinant $1$
The set of all $n\times n$ real matrices under addition
The unit circle $S^1$ under complex multiplication
hard.hint: Which option hides a non-Lie structure or dimension issue?
hard.explain: The set $M_n(\mathbb{R})$ under addition is a Lie group (a vector group), so that is fine. The intended distractor is none — all four are Lie groups. Revisiting: the real point is to test that $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is open in $M_n$ (hence smooth), $\mathrm{SL}_n$ is a closed submanifold, $M_n$ under addition is $\mathbb{R}^{n^2}$, and $S^1\subset\mathbb{C}^*$ is a Lie group. The only non-example from a standard list would be a discrete group lacking a smooth structure, but all four options here are valid Lie groups.

hard.q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{U}(n)$ for $n=3$?
hard.hint: Real dimension of $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is $n^2$.
hard.explain: $\mathrm{U}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2$. For $n=3$: $3^2=9$. This follows because a unitary matrix satisfies $U^\dagger U=I$, giving $n^2$ real constraints on $2n^2$ real parameters, leaving $n^2$ free dimensions.

hard.q: To verify $G=\{A\in M_2(\mathbb{R})\mid A^\top A=I,\,\det A=1\}=\mathrm{SO}(2)$ is a Lie group, which is the correct sequence of steps?
choices: Show $G$ is a closed subgroup of $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})$, then apply the closed-subgroup theorem
Triangulate $G$ as a CW complex and verify the group axioms combinatorially
Embed $G$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$ and apply the Whitney embedding theorem
Verify $G$ is compact and then use the Peter–Weyl theorem
hard.hint: Closed-subgroup theorem gives smooth structure for free.
hard.explain: The closed-subgroup theorem (Cartan's theorem) states that any closed subgroup of a Lie group is itself a Lie group. Since $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ is defined by continuous equations inside $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})$, it is closed, and the theorem applies directly.

lie-algebra-tangent.title: Lie algebra as tangent space at the identity

questions.q: For a closed matrix Lie group $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$, its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ is computed as:
choices: the set of all $n\times n$ real matrices
the set of $X\in M_n(\mathbb{R})$ arising as $A'(0)$ for some smooth curve $A(t)\subset G$ with $A(0)=I$
the set of invertible matrices in $G$
the quotient $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})/G$
questions.explain: By definition $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ is the space of velocity vectors at the identity. Concretely, differentiate a curve through $I$ lying in $G$ and collect all such tangent vectors. This recovers $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ (trace zero), $\mathfrak{so}(n)$ (skew-symmetric), etc.

questions.q: Differentiating $A(t)^\top A(t)=I$ at $t=0$ with $A(0)=I$ gives which equation on $X=A'(0)$? Enter the answer as the value of $(X^\top+X)_{1,1}$ when $X$ satisfies this equation.
questions.explain: Differentiation yields $X^\top+X=0$, i.e. $X$ is skew-symmetric, so every diagonal entry vanishes. In particular $(X^\top+X)_{1,1}=2X_{1,1}=0$.

questions.q: What is the real dimension of $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{R})$ (the traceless $3\times 3$ real matrices)?
questions.explain: $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ is cut out of $M_n\cong\mathbb{R}^{n^2}$ by the single linear condition $\operatorname{tr}X=0$, so $\dim\mathfrak{sl}_n=n^2-1$. For $n=3$: $9-1=8$.

hard.q: A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is an abstract vector space with a bracket $[\,,\,]$. Which list of axioms characterises it?
choices: bilinear, symmetric, Jacobi identity
bilinear, antisymmetric, associative
bilinear, antisymmetric ($[X,X]=0$), Jacobi identity $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$
bilinear, commutes with scalar multiplication, has a unit element
hard.hint: Lie brackets replace associativity with Jacobi.
hard.explain: A Lie algebra is a vector space with a bilinear bracket satisfying $[X,X]=0$ (antisymmetry in characteristic $\ne 2$) and the Jacobi identity. Lie brackets are never associative; Jacobi is the "weak" associativity replacement.

hard.q: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$ (the cross product). Compute the second component of $[E_1,E_2]+[E_2,E_3]+[E_3,E_1]$ in the $E_1,E_2,E_3$ basis.
hard.hint: Use $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ directly; don't confuse with Jacobi.
hard.explain: $[E_1,E_2]=E_3$, $[E_2,E_3]=E_1$, $[E_3,E_1]=E_2$. Sum is $E_1+E_2+E_3$, whose second component (coefficient of $E_2$) is $1$. Notice this is NOT zero — Jacobi $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$ applies to triple brackets, not iterated pairwise brackets.

hard.q: Lie's correspondence associates to each real Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ exactly one simply-connected Lie group $\widetilde G$. Which pair illustrates that the non-simply-connected $G$ is NOT uniquely determined by $\mathfrak{g}$?
choices: $\mathfrak{u}(1)$ corresponds both to $\mathbb{R}$ (simply connected) and $S^1=\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ (a quotient by a discrete subgroup)
$\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{R})$ corresponds uniquely to $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$
$\mathfrak{su}(2)$ corresponds uniquely to $\mathrm{SU}(2)=S^3$
The zero Lie algebra corresponds to every finite group
hard.hint: Find two groups with the same abelian algebra but different $\pi_1$.
hard.explain: Both $\mathbb{R}$ and $S^1$ have Lie algebra $\mathfrak{u}(1)\cong\mathbb{R}$ (abelian, 1-dim). The simply-connected representative is $\mathbb{R}$; the quotient $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}=S^1$ has the same algebra. In general a Lie algebra determines a Lie group only up to its universal cover and discrete central quotients.

questions.q: For a matrix Lie group $G$, the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ carries a bracket given by:
choices: the ordinary matrix product $XY$
the commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$
the anticommutator $XY+YX$
the Frobenius inner product $\mathrm{tr}(X^\top Y)$
questions.explain: For matrix groups the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{g}$ is the matrix commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$, which is automatically antisymmetric and satisfies the Jacobi identity.

questions.q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\theta\\\theta&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{so}(2)$ with $\theta=\pi$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $\exp(X)$.
questions.explain: $\exp\begin{pmatrix}0&-\theta\\\theta&0\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}$. At $\theta=\pi$ the $(1,1)$ entry is $\cos\pi=-1$.

questions.q: If $X\in\mathfrak{sl}_n(\mathbb{R})$ has $\operatorname{tr}(X)=0$, what is $\det(\exp X)$?
questions.explain: The identity $\det(e^X)=e^{\operatorname{tr}(X)}$ holds for any square matrix. With $\operatorname{tr} X=0$ we get $\det(\exp X)=e^0=1$, confirming $\exp$ maps $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ into $\mathrm{SL}_n$.

hard.q: The Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff formula states $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(Z)$ where $Z$ begins $X+Y+\frac{1}{2}[X,Y]+\cdots$. If $[X,Y]=0$, which conclusion follows?
choices: $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(X+Y)$, so the exponential is a group homomorphism on the commutative subalgebra
$\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(X)\exp(Y)$ with no simplification possible
$\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp([X,Y])=\exp(0)=I$ always
$\exp(X)$ and $\exp(Y)$ anticommute
hard.hint: Commuting matrices collapse all BCH correction terms.
hard.explain: When $[X,Y]=0$ all higher BCH terms also vanish (they are built from nested brackets), so $Z=X+Y$ and $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(X+Y)$. This is why the exponential is a homomorphism from any abelian Lie algebra to the corresponding abelian Lie group.

hard.q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{gl}_2(\mathbb{R})$. Compute the $(1,2)$ entry of $\exp(X)$.
hard.hint: This $X$ is nilpotent with $X^2=0$.
hard.explain: $X^2=0$, so $\exp(X)=I+X=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$. The $(1,2)$ entry is $1$.

hard.q: For a compact connected Lie group $G$, which statement about $\exp:\mathfrak{g}\to G$ is true?
choices: $\exp$ is always injective
$\exp$ is surjective but generally not injective
$\exp$ is a diffeomorphism near the identity only if $G$ is abelian
$\exp$ is never surjective for non-abelian groups
hard.hint: Compact connected forces onto, but periodicity breaks injectivity.
hard.explain: For compact connected Lie groups, every element lies on a one-parameter subgroup, so $\exp$ is surjective. Injectivity fails: e.g., in $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$, the matrices $\exp(0)=I$ and $\exp(2\pi iH)=I$ for suitable $H$ show different algebra elements mapping to the same group element.

questions.q: The group-commutator/bracket identity $\exp(tX)\exp(tY)\exp(-tX)\exp(-tY)=\exp\!\big(t^2[X,Y]+O(t^3)\big)$ tells us the leading nonzero order is $t^2$ because:
choices: the first-order term $X+Y-X-Y$ already vanishes, so the next surviving contribution is the antisymmetric piece $XY-YX$
the exponential series has no linear term
$\exp(tX)\exp(tY)$ always equals $\exp(t(X+Y))$
BCH converges only at order $t^2$
questions.explain: Expanding each exponential to second order and collecting: the $O(t)$ piece is $X+Y-X-Y=0$ and the symmetric $O(t^2)$ piece ($\tfrac12 X^2$ etc.) cancels in the four-fold product; what survives is $t^2(XY-YX)=t^2[X,Y]$.
questions.hint: Expand each exp to second order, then look at what the four-fold product keeps vs. cancels.

questions.q: In $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$, compute the coefficient of $E_2$ in $[E_1,[E_2,E_3]]+[E_2,[E_3,E_1]]+[E_3,[E_1,E_2]]$.
questions.explain: The Jacobi identity forces this sum to be zero, so every coefficient — including that of $E_2$ — vanishes. Concretely: $[E_2,E_3]=E_1$ so $[E_1,E_1]=0$; $[E_3,E_1]=E_2$ so $[E_2,E_2]=0$; $[E_1,E_2]=E_3$ so $[E_3,E_3]=0$. Sum $=0$.

questions.q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $Y=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ in $\mathfrak{sl}_2$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $[X,Y]$.
questions.explain: $XY=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $YX=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$, so $[X,Y]=XY-YX=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}=h$. The $(1,1)$ entry is $1$ — this recovers the $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ relation $[e,f]=h$.

hard.q: The Jacobi identity $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$ is equivalent to the assertion that:
choices: the bracket is associative
$\operatorname{ad}_X=[X,\cdot]$ is a derivation of the bracket: $\operatorname{ad}_X[Y,Z]=[\operatorname{ad}_X Y,Z]+[Y,\operatorname{ad}_X Z]$
every bracket $[X,Y]$ equals zero
$\mathfrak{g}$ is commutative
hard.hint: Rewrite Jacobi as a Leibniz rule for $\operatorname{ad}_X$.
hard.explain: Rearranging Jacobi: $[X,[Y,Z]]=[[X,Y],Z]+[Y,[X,Z]]$, which is exactly the Leibniz rule stating $\operatorname{ad}_X$ differentiates the bracket. This is the conceptual content of Jacobi — it is the 'weak associativity' replacement that makes $\operatorname{ad}$ a Lie algebra homomorphism into $\operatorname{Der}(\mathfrak{g})$.

hard.q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with standard basis $h,e,f$ and relations $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute the coefficient of $e$ in the BCH term $\tfrac{1}{12}\big([X,[X,Y]]+[Y,[Y,X]]\big)$ when $X=h$ and $Y=e$.
hard.hint: Compute the two nested brackets, then multiply by $1/12$.
hard.explain: $[h,e]=2e$, so $[h,[h,e]]=[h,2e]=4e$. $[e,[e,h]]=[e,-2e]=0$. Sum is $4e+0=4e$. Multiplying by $\tfrac{1}{12}$: $\tfrac{4}{12}e=\tfrac{1}{3}e$. Coefficient $=1/3\approx0.3333$.

hard.q: Two smooth vector fields $V,W$ on a Lie group $G$ that are left-invariant are determined by their values at $e$, giving $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$. How does the Lie bracket of vector fields recover the matrix commutator for $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n$?
choices: they are unrelated — vector-field brackets and matrix commutators are distinct structures
the Lie bracket $[V,W]$ of left-invariant vector fields is again left-invariant, and its value at $e$ equals $XY-YX$ where $X=V_e,Y=W_e$; this defines the abstract bracket on $\mathfrak{g}$
the bracket is the Poisson bracket of Hamiltonians on $T^*G$
one must first complexify $\mathfrak{g}$ before the two agree
hard.hint: Left-invariant fields identify with $T_eG$; their bracket collapses to the commutator.
hard.explain: Left-invariant vector fields form a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{X}(G)$ under the vector-field bracket, canonically identified with $T_eG$ via $V\mapsto V_e$. Computing in local coordinates on a matrix group, the vector-field bracket at $e$ reduces to the matrix commutator $XY-YX$, unifying the manifold-theoretic and matrix-theoretic definitions of the Lie bracket.

so3-su2-double-cover.title: SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover

questions.q: The covering homomorphism $\pi:\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has kernel:
choices: trivial: $\pi$ is an isomorphism
$\{\pm I\}\subset\mathrm{SU}(2)$
the full centre $\mathrm{U}(1)\subset\mathrm{SU}(2)$
$\mathbb{Z}/4$
questions.explain: The quaternion action $q\cdot\vec v=q\vec v q^{-1}$ on imaginary quaternions is an element of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$, and $\pm q$ act identically because the action is conjugation. Hence $\ker\pi=\{\pm I\}\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$.

questions.q: Let $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\mathbf{i}$ be the unit quaternion implementing rotation by $\theta$ about the $x$-axis. At what value of $\theta$ (in radians) does $q$ first equal $-1$?
questions.explain: Set $\cos(\theta/2)=-1$: minimal solution is $\theta/2=\pi$, i.e. $\theta=2\pi$. A single $360°$ rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ lifts to the path $I\to -I$ in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ — the spinor sign.

questions.q: Which of these statements about the pair $(\mathrm{SU}(2),\mathrm{SO}(3))$ is TRUE?
choices: $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is simply connected; $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ is not
$\mathrm{SU}(2)$ is simply connected ($\pi_1=0$); $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}/2$
Both groups have the same fundamental group $\mathbb{Z}$
Both groups are simply connected
questions.explain: $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$ is simply connected. The antipodal quotient $S^3/\{\pm1\}=\mathbb{RP}^3\cong\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}/2$; this non-trivial loop is the famous "belt trick."

hard.q: Two Lie groups share the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{su}(2)\cong\mathfrak{so}(3)$ (both 3-dim with cross-product bracket). Which conclusion follows?
choices: $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ and $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are isomorphic as Lie groups
$\mathrm{SU}(2)$ is the simply-connected cover; $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is a quotient by a discrete central subgroup, and Lie's correspondence places both in the same germ at the identity
The exponential maps $\exp:\mathfrak{su}(2)\to\mathrm{SU}(2)$ and $\exp:\mathfrak{so}(3)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are equal as set maps
$\mathfrak{so}(3)$ must be abelian since $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has finite fundamental group
hard.hint: Same algebra means same germ, differing only by discrete central quotients.
hard.explain: Lie's third theorem gives a unique simply-connected group per Lie algebra ($\mathrm{SU}(2)$ here); other connected Lie groups with the same algebra are quotients by discrete central subgroups. $\mathrm{SO}(3)=\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm I\}$ realises this.

hard.q: A spin-$\tfrac12$ representation of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ does not descend to $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ because $-I\in\ker\pi$ acts by what scalar on the spin-$\tfrac12$ space?
hard.hint: Half-integer spin picks up a sign under the $-I$ covering element.
hard.explain: The spin-$\tfrac12$ rep is the defining rep on $\mathbb{C}^2$: $-I\cdot v=-v$. For descent to $\mathrm{SO}(3)=\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm I\}$ we would need $-I$ to act trivially (scalar $+1$), but it acts by $-1$. Only integer-spin reps descend; half-integer spin lives on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ only — this is the spinor/fermion distinction.

hard.q: The map $q\mapsto (\vec v\mapsto q\vec v q^{-1})$ sends a unit quaternion $q\in S^3$ to a rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$. Which sequence of steps shows this is a 2:1 covering homomorphism?
choices: (i) Verify $q\vec v q^{-1}\in\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^3$; (ii) check it preserves the norm; (iii) compute $\det=+1$; (iv) identify the kernel $\{q:q\vec v q^{-1}=\vec v\;\forall\vec v\}=\{\pm 1\}$
(i) Whitney embed $S^3$ into $\mathbb{R}^7$; (ii) apply Sard's theorem; (iii) project
(i) Diagonalise every $q$; (ii) read off rotation angle; (iii) assert 2:1 by character theory
(i) Restrict $q$ to the equator $S^2\subset S^3$; (ii) note this is a 2:1 map to $\mathbb{RP}^2$
hard.hint: Check invariance of $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$, norm, determinant, then the kernel.
hard.explain: The standard verification: show conjugation preserves imaginary quaternions (identified with $\mathbb{R}^3$), preserves the quadratic form (norm), has determinant $+1$ (by connectedness of $S^3$), and identify the kernel. The centre of the quaternion algebra acting trivially by conjugation is exactly $\{\pm 1\}$.

questions.q: The adjoint representation of a Lie group $G$ on its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is:
choices: left multiplication: $L_g(X)=gX$
conjugation: $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=gXg^{-1}$
the exponential: $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=\exp(X)$
the commutator: $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=gX-Xg$
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is conjugation, $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=gXg^{-1}$ for matrix groups. It is a homomorphism because $\mathrm{Ad}_{gh}=\mathrm{Ad}_g\mathrm{Ad}_h$.

questions.q: Differentiating $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ at the identity yields the map $\operatorname{ad}:\mathfrak{g}\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak{g})$ given by:
choices: $\operatorname{ad}_X(Y)=XY$
$\operatorname{ad}_X(Y)=[X,Y]$
$\operatorname{ad}_X(Y)=\exp(X)Y\exp(-X)$
$\operatorname{ad}_X(Y)=X+Y$
questions.explain: Chain rule: $\frac{d}{dt}\big|_0 e^{tX}Ye^{-tX}=XY-YX=[X,Y]$. So the differential of $\mathrm{Ad}$ at $e$ is the adjoint action $\operatorname{ad}_X=[X,\cdot]$ on the algebra.

questions.q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ and $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute $B(h,h)=\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{ad}_h\circ\operatorname{ad}_h)$ (the Killing form value).
questions.explain: $\operatorname{ad}_h$ acts on $h,e,f$ with eigenvalues $0,2,-2$. Hence $\operatorname{ad}_h^2$ has eigenvalues $0,4,4$, and the trace is $0+4+4=8$.

hard.q: Cartan's criterion characterises semisimplicity via the Killing form. Which statement is correct?
choices: $\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple $\iff$ $B$ is positive definite
$\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple $\iff$ $B$ is non-degenerate
$\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple $\iff$ $B\equiv 0$
$\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple $\iff$ $B$ is symmetric
hard.hint: Cartan's criterion is non-degeneracy, not sign-definiteness.
hard.explain: Cartan's criterion: a finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field of characteristic $0$ is semisimple iff its Killing form is non-degenerate. For compact real forms $B$ is negative definite (stronger than non-degenerate).

hard.q: On $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ the Killing form $B$ turns out to be a negative multiple of the Euclidean inner product on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Which conclusion about the corresponding group $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ follows?
choices: $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is non-compact because $B$ is not positive
$\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is compact because the negative-definite $-B$ gives a bi-invariant Riemannian metric with bounded volume
$\mathfrak{so}(3)$ is solvable
$\mathrm{SO}(3)$ admits no invariant metric
hard.hint: Negative-definite $B$ signals Weyl's compactness criterion.
hard.explain: Negative-definite Killing form $\Leftrightarrow$ the real Lie group is compact (among connected semisimple groups, this is Weyl's criterion). $-B$ is a bi-invariant positive-definite inner product on $\mathfrak{so}(3)$, extending to a bi-invariant Riemannian metric on $\mathrm{SO}(3)$; compactness follows.

hard.q: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ as above. The adjoint orbit of $h$ under $\mathrm{SL}_2$ consists of all matrices with the same Jordan type as $h$. What is the dimension of this orbit?
hard.hint: Orbit dimension equals $\dim G$ minus stabiliser dimension.
hard.explain: The stabiliser of $h$ is the diagonal torus $\{\operatorname{diag}(a,a^{-1})\}$, which is $1$-dimensional. Hence $\dim(\mathrm{Ad}\text{-orbit})=\dim\mathrm{SL}_2-\dim\mathrm{stab}=3-1=2$. Geometrically it is the one-sheeted hyperboloid $\{X\in\mathfrak{sl}_2:\det X=-1\}$.

questions.q: The adjoint action $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is defined by:
choices: $\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=gX$ (left multiplication on the algebra)
$\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=gXg^{-1}$ (conjugation extended to $\mathfrak{g}$)
$\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=[g,X]$ (commutator with a group element)
$\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=\exp(X)g$
questions.explain: The adjoint representation is conjugation: $\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=gXg^{-1}$ for matrix groups. Its differential at $e$ is $\operatorname{ad}_X(Y)=[X,Y]$.

questions.q: How many roots does the root system $A_2$ (that is, $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{C})$) have?
questions.explain: The roots of $A_n$ are $\varepsilon_i-\varepsilon_j$ for $i\ne j$ in $\{1,\dots,n+1\}$, giving $(n+1)n$ roots. For $A_2$: $3\cdot 2=6$.

questions.q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ satisfying $[h,e]=2e$, what is the eigenvalue of $\operatorname{ad}_h$ on $e$?
questions.explain: $\operatorname{ad}_h(e)=[h,e]=2e$, so $e$ is an eigenvector of $\operatorname{ad}_h$ with eigenvalue $2$. This eigenvalue is the root $\alpha(h)=2$ for $A_1$.

hard.q: The Killing form $B(X,Y)=\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{ad}_X\circ\operatorname{ad}_Y)$ is used to classify semisimple Lie algebras. Which criterion uses it directly?
choices: Cartan's criterion: $\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple if and only if $B$ is non-degenerate
Cartan's criterion: $\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple if and only if $B$ is positive definite
The Killing form is always degenerate for compact groups
A Lie algebra is solvable if and only if $B$ is non-degenerate
hard.hint: Non-degeneracy, not sign, is the semisimplicity test.
hard.explain: Cartan's semisimplicity criterion states $\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple iff the Killing form is non-degenerate. For compact real forms the Killing form is negative definite (a stronger condition), but non-degeneracy is the precise characterization of semisimplicity.

hard.q: How many positive roots does the root system $B_2$ (equivalently $\mathfrak{so}(5)$ or $\mathfrak{sp}(4)$) have?
hard.hint: Half of $n^2$ roots are positive in $B_n$.
hard.explain: $B_2$ has $8$ roots in total ($4$ positive, $4$ negative). The positive roots are $\varepsilon_1,\varepsilon_2,\varepsilon_1-\varepsilon_2,\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2$. In general $B_n$ has $n^2$ roots, giving $4$ for $n=2$, so $4$ positive roots.

hard.q: The correct order of steps to derive the root-space decomposition of a semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is:
choices: Choose a Borel subalgebra → define roots as weights of the adjoint action on $\mathfrak{g}$ → recover $\mathfrak{h}$

Choose a maximal toral subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ → simultaneously diagonalise $\operatorname{ad}_H$ for $H\in\mathfrak{h}$ → define roots $\alpha\in\mathfrak{h}^*$ as nonzero simultaneous eigenvalues → decompose $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{h}\oplus\bigoplus_\alpha\mathfrak{g}_\alpha$
Pick any element $X\in\mathfrak{g}$ → exponentiate → read off roots from the spectrum of $\exp(X)$
Compute the center of $\mathfrak{g}$ → quotient by it → apply Weyl's theorem
hard.hint: Start from a Cartan subalgebra and simultaneously diagonalise $\operatorname{ad}$.
hard.explain: The standard construction: (1) fix a maximal toral (Cartan) subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$; (2) the elements of $\mathfrak{h}$ act semisimply via $\operatorname{ad}$, so $\mathfrak{g}$ decomposes into simultaneous eigenspaces; (3) the nonzero eigenvalues are the roots $\alpha\in\mathfrak{h}^*$; (4) each root space $\mathfrak{g}_\alpha=\{X\in\mathfrak{g}\mid[H,X]=\alpha(H)X\;\forall H\in\mathfrak{h}\}$ is one-dimensional for semisimple $\mathfrak{g}$.
